content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


May 20, 1983


Elizabeth Strauss
Napa City Attorney
P.O. Box 660
Napa, CA 94559

                                        Re: Your Request For Advice, Our
                                            Advice No. A-83-133

Dear Ms. Strauss:

     Bob Stern has referred your letter to the Legal Division for
a response. You have asked whether Mayor Phillis Moore may
participate in city council decisions on:

     1. An application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density
designation change in the City of Napa General Plan. Mayor
Moore's adult son has been hired to act as a consultant to Mr.
and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project for which the
density change is sought.

     2. A proposal to amend the General Plan density designation
for a parcel of vacant property located next to a condominium in
which the Mayor's residence is located.

     Government Code Section 87100 1/ requires a public official
to disqualify herself from voting on, or participating in, any
decision in which she has a financial interest. Section 87103
defines the term financial interests:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
          official has a direct or indirect investment worth
          more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

___________________
     1/ All statutory references made are to the
Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Elizabeth Strauss
May 20, 1983
page 2


(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

The facts you have presented in your first question do not,
by themselves, indicate any potential conflict of interest under
the Act. If the Mayor's only connection with the proposed
density designation charge is the fact that her adult son is a
consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills, she does not have to
disqualify herself from the decision on the charge. The Act
does not require disqualification of an official from a decision
merely because the decision may have a financial effect on the
official's adult child.

The second situation described in your advice request does
present a potential conflict of interest. From the facts, I
assume that Mayor Moore has an investment interest of over
$1,000 in her condominium and in the common area in which the
condominium development is located. This constitutes an
interest in real property within the meaning of the Act.
Section 82033. Therefore, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the proposed change in the density designation of the adjacent
city-owned parcel will have a material financial effect on the
value of her condominium, or on the value of the common area
owned by condominium development, she must disqualify herself
from that decision.

From the facts you have provided, I am unable to determine
whether or not the proposed density change will have a material
financial effect on the value of the Mayor's condominium, or on
the value of the common area owned by the condominium
development. I suggest that you consult Commission regulation
18702(b)(2) (copy enclosed) which explains what constitutes a

Elizabeth Strauss
May 20, 1983
Page 3


material financial effect on an interest in real property. You
may want to consult some local real estate brokers, or the City
or County Assessor, if you are in doubt as to the probable
effect of the proposed density designation change on the
adjacent property.

     I hope this answers your questions.  Please feel free to
call if you want any further advice on this matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief, Legal Division

BAM:km
Enclosure

City of NAPA                                    FPPC                    COMMUNITY SERVICES BUILDING
                                                         MAY 13 9 00 AH '03      1600 FIRST STREET, P.O. BOX 660
                                                                                 NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94559-0660
                                                                                 (707) 252-7711


                                                                    May 16, 1983


Robert Stern
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
100 "K" Street Building
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Bob:

     In my capacity as City Attorney of the City of Napa, Mayor Phyllis Moore asked
that I ask for an advice letter in response to the assertion that she should refrain from
participating in two matters currently before the City Council because of a "conflict
of interest".

     Each of these matters will be discussed by the City Council on May 31, 1983.
Your advice prior to that date would facilitate the Mayor's action on this matter.

     Thank you very much for your attention. If you need additional facts or
clarification about either of these matters, please give me a call.

                                                         Best regards,


                                                         [signature]

                                                         Elizabeth Strauss
                                                         CITY ATTORNEY

ESselg/wp

Advice Letter re: Phyllis Moore, Mayor of the City of Napa, California


An assertion has been made that Mayor Phyllis Moore should refrain from
participating in the decisions on two matters to be discussed by the City Council of
the City of Napa on May 31, 1983.

Matter #1

Mr. and Mrs. Hills own property within the City of Napa with a General Plan
density designation of High Residential, 12 - 25 units per acre. The General Plan also
allows under certain circumstances, property designated High Residential, additional
density ("special residential density") to accommodate housing for low and moderate
income seniors. Mr. and Mrs. Hills wish to develop their property for low and
moderate income seniors and therefore are asking the City of Napa to allow the
special residential density for their property and to make certain other discretionary
land - use decisions (rezoning, use permit) approving for the proposed development.

Mr. and Mrs. Hills have retained Mr. Jeff Moore as their consultant to process
their application through the City of Napa. Mr. Moore's services include completing
an application form, drafting materials describing the project, meeting with neighbors
of the project and appearing at City Planning Commission and City Council meetings
to present the merits of the project. Mr. Moore does not have a financial interest in
the project but rather, his fee for his consultant services is comprised of an hourly fee
plus a percentage of the project construction costs. He does not and will not have an
ownership interest in the project. Mr. Moore is over twenty-one years of age and is
Mayor Phyllis Moore's son.

In addition to the Hills' proposal for this property, a Councilmember has proposed
that the General Plan designation for this property be amended to permit 6 - 12 units
per acre. (In addition to changing the general density, this proposal would preclude the
"special residential density" request by the Hills). The Councilmember's proposal
will be discussed on May 31, 1983. It has been asserted that Mayor Moore should not
participate in this discussion because of her son's involvement in the Hills' proposal.

Matter #2

The City of Napa owns a parcel of vacant property consisting of approximately
1.43 acres on Clay Street. The property has a General Plan density designation of
Medium Residential, 6 - 12 units per acre. A Councilmember has proposed that the
General Plan designation for this property be amended to High Residential, 12 - 25
units per acre.

Mayor Phyllis Moore owns a condominium (her residence) in a condominium
development constructed on a four-acre parcel of land. The development is located on
the parcel directly west of the City-owned parcel on Clay Street. Within the
development, the Mayor's residence is one-tenth of one mile from City-owned
parcel. In addition to her residence, Mayor Moore owns an undeveloped interest (less
than ten percent) in the common area within the development. The condominium
development is built to a density of approximately ten units per acre. The
neighborhood most immediately surrounding these parcels is developed at a density of
6 - 12 units per acre.

The Councilmember's proposal to amend the General Plan to designate the City-
owned parcel High Residential, 12 - 25 units per acre, will be discussed on May 31,
1983. It has been asserted that Mayor Moore should not participate in this discussion
because of her ownership interest in her residence and in the common area within the
condominium development.

sections.question: 1. Whether Mayor Phyllis Moore may participate in city council decisions regarding an application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density designation change, where Mayor Moore's adult son has been hired as a consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project. 2. Whether Mayor Moore may participate in a proposal to amend the General Plan density designation for a parcel of vacant property located next to a condominium in which the Mayor's residence is located.
sections.conclusion: On the first question, the Mayor does not have to disqualify herself from the decision on the density designation change, as the Act does not require disqualification merely because a decision may have a financial effect on an official's adult child. On the second question, the Mayor must disqualify herself if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed density change will have a material financial effect on the value of her condominium or the common area of the condominium development. The requester should consult Commission regulation 18702(b)(2) and potentially local real estate brokers or the City or County Assessor to determine whether a material financial effect is probable.
sections.question_synthetic: May Mayor Phyllis Moore participate in two city council decisions: (1) approving a density designation change for property where her adult son is acting as a consultant, and (2) amending the density designation of adjacent vacant land next to her condominium?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Mayor may participate in the first decision regarding her son's consultant work, as the Act does not require disqualification based on effects to adult children. However, she must disqualify herself from the second decision regarding the adjacent property if the density change would have a material financial effect on her condominium's value, which requires further factual analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of incoming request letter (May 16, 1983) followed by FPPC response letter (May 20, 1983) from Barbara A. Milman, Chief of Legal Division. Both sections extracted; FPPC advice letter is primary document analyzed. Minor OCR errors in original document (e.g., 'particlpating' for 'participating') preserved in extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Whether Mayor Phyllis Moore may participate in city council decisions regarding an application by Mr. and Mrs. Hills for a density designation change, where Mayor Moore's adult son has been hired as a consultant to Mr. and Mrs. Hills in connection with the project. 2. Whether Mayor Moore may participate in a proposal to amend the General Plan density designation for a parcel of vacant property located next to a condominium in which the Mayor's residence is located.

CONCLUSION: On the first question, the Mayor does not have to disqualify herself from the decision on the density designation change, as the Act does not require disqualification merely because a decision may have a financial effect on an official's adult child. On the second question, the Mayor must disqualify herself if it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed density change will have a material financial effect on the value of her condominium or the common area of the condominium development. The requester should consult Commission regulation 18702(b)(2) and potentially local real estate brokers or the City or County Assessor to determine whether a material financial effect is probable.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Mayor Phyllis Moore need not disqualify herself from a city council decision regarding a density designation change where her adult son serves as a consultant, but must disqualify herself from voting on amending the density designation of vacant property adjacent to her condominium if the change would materially affect her property's value.
